entries.name: St. Mary's Cathedral
entries.desc: A Gothic Revival cathedral in sandstone, the seat of Sydney's Catholic archdiocese. Its twin spires were finally completed in 2000, long after the main building.

photo.alt: St. Mary's Cathedral illuminated at night, its twin Gothic spires lit gold
photo.credit: <!-- photo: st-marys-cathedral-sydney.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 2.5 · "St Mary's Cathedral, Sydney HDR" by Adam.J.W.C. · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:St_Mary%27s_Cathedral,_Sydney_HDR.jpg -->

entries.name: St. Stephen's Cathedral (Stephansdom)
entries.desc: A Gothic landmark at the heart of Vienna, distinguished by its ornately tiled roof and towering south spire. Catacombs beneath hold the remains of Habsburg royalty.

photo.alt: St. Stephen's Cathedral in Vienna, showing its patterned tiled roof and towering south spire
photo.credit: <!-- photo: st-stephens-cathedral-vienna.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 4.0 · "Wien - Stephansdom (2)" by C.Stadler/Bwag · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Wien_-_Stephansdom_(2).JPG -->

entries.name: Salzburg Cathedral
entries.desc: An early Baroque cathedral in Salzburg's Old Town, notable as the church where Mozart was baptized and later served as organist.

photo.alt: Salzburg Cathedral's Baroque facade with twin green-domed bell towers
photo.credit: <!-- photo: salzburg-cathedral.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 4.0 · "Salzburg Dom Fassade 01" by Uoaei1 · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Salzburg_Dom_Fassade_01.jpg -->

entries.name: Cathedral of St. Michael and St. Gudula
entries.desc: A Brabantine Gothic cathedral with twin towers, serving as the setting for Belgian royal weddings and state funerals. It is dedicated to the patron saints of Brussels.

photo.alt: Cathedral of St. Michael and St. Gudula's twin Gothic towers in Brussels
photo.credit: <!-- photo: st-michael-st-gudula-cathedral-brussels.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 4.0 · "Brussels West facade of the Cathedral of St. Michael and St. Gudula" by Ymblanter · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Brussels_West_facade_of_the_Cathedral_of_St._Michael_and_St._Gudula.jpg -->

entries.name: São Paulo Cathedral (Catedral da Sé)
entries.desc: A large Neo-Gothic cathedral in central São Paulo, one of the biggest churches in Brazil. Its crypt holds the tombs of notable figures from the city's history.

photo.alt: São Paulo Cathedral's Neo-Gothic twin towers rising above Praça da Sé, lined by palm trees
photo.credit: <!-- photo: se-cathedral-sao-paulo.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC0 · "Catedral da Sé em São Paulo" by Wilfredor · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Catedral_da_S%C3%A9_em_S%C3%A3o_Paulo.jpg -->

entries.name: Cathedral of Brasília
entries.desc: A Modernist cathedral designed by Oscar Niemeyer, formed of sweeping curved concrete columns that create a crown-like silhouette and a glass-walled, light-filled interior.

photo.alt: Cathedral of Brasília's hyperboloid concrete crown
photo.credit: <!-- photo: cathedral-of-brasilia.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 4.0 · "Catedral Metropolitana de Brasilia.jpg" by Tissiana de A. de Souza · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Catedral_Metropolitana_de_Brasilia.jpg -->

entries.name: Metropolitan Cathedral of Rio de Janeiro
entries.desc: A Modernist cathedral shaped like a truncated cone, inspired by Mayan pyramids. Four floor-to-ceiling stained-glass strips flood the vast interior with color.

photo.alt: Interior of the Metropolitan Cathedral of Rio de Janeiro, its four stained-glass strips rising to the apex
photo.credit: <!-- photo: metropolitan-cathedral-rio-interior.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 4.0 · "Rio de janeiro cathedral sao sebastiao 2010" by chensiyuan · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Rio_de_janeiro_cathedral_sao_sebastiao_2010.JPG -->

entries.name: Alexander Nevsky Cathedral
entries.desc: A large Neo-Byzantine Orthodox cathedral topped with gilded domes, built to honor Russian soldiers who died in the war that freed Bulgaria from Ottoman rule.

photo.alt: Alexander Nevsky Cathedral's gilded and green domes above Sofia's central square
photo.credit: <!-- photo: alexander-nevsky-cathedral-sofia.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 3.0 · "Alexander Nevsky Cathedral, Sofia (by Pudelek)" by Pudelek (Marcin Szala) · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Alexander_Nevsky_Cathedral,_Sofia_(by_Pudelek).JPG -->

entries.name: Notre-Dame Basilica of Montreal
entries.desc: A Gothic Revival basilica in Old Montreal, famous for its deep-blue vaulted ceiling studded with gold stars and its intricately carved interior.

photo.alt: Notre-Dame Basilica exterior in Old Montreal
photo.credit: <!-- photo: notre-dame-basilica-montreal.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 4.0 · "Basilique Notre-Dame de Montréal, extérieur 01.jpg" by Pierre5018 · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Basilique_Notre-Dame_de_Montr%C3%A9al,_ext%C3%A9rieur_01.jpg -->

entries.name: Las Lajas Sanctuary
entries.desc: A Gothic Revival church built into a steep river gorge and spanning it on a tall bridge. A pilgrimage site tied to a reported Marian apparition on the canyon wall.

photo.alt: Las Lajas Sanctuary spanning the Guáitara river gorge
photo.credit: <!-- photo: las-lajas-sanctuary.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 4.0 · "Santuario de Las Lajas, Ipiales, Colombia, 2015-07-21, DD 01.JPG" by Diego Delso · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Santuario_de_Las_Lajas,_Ipiales,_Colombia,_2015-07-21,_DD_01.JPG -->

entries.name: Cathedral of Saint Domnius
entries.desc: Built within the Roman mausoleum of Emperor Diocletian, it is considered the oldest cathedral building still in use. It sits at the core of Split's ancient palace.

photo.alt: Cathedral of Saint Domnius and bell tower, Split
photo.credit: <!-- photo: cathedral-of-saint-domnius-split.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 3.0 · "Cathedral of Saint Domnius.jpg" by Mattias Hill · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Cathedral_of_Saint_Domnius.jpg -->

entries.name: St. Vitus Cathedral
entries.desc: A Gothic cathedral within the Prague Castle complex, the spiritual heart of Bohemia. It holds the tombs of Bohemian kings and the country's crown jewels.

photo.alt: St. Vitus Cathedral within Prague Castle
photo.credit: <!-- photo: st-vitus-cathedral-prague.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 4.0 · "Exterior of St. Vitus Cathedral Prague 8.JPG" by Serafita · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Exterior_of_St._Vitus_Cathedral_Prague_8.JPG -->

entries.name: Church of Our Saviour (Vor Frelsers Kirke)
entries.desc: A Baroque church in Copenhagen best known for its external spiral staircase winding up the spire, which visitors can climb for views over the city.

photo.alt: Church of Our Saviour and its spiral spire, Copenhagen
photo.credit: <!-- photo: church-of-our-saviour-copenhagen.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 4.0 · "Church of our Saviour, Copenhagen.jpg" by John Samuel · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Church_of_our_Saviour,_Copenhagen.jpg -->

entries.name: Helsinki Cathedral
entries.desc: A white Neoclassical Lutheran cathedral overlooking Senate Square, designed by Carl Ludvig Engel. Its green domes are among the most recognizable images of the city.

photo.alt: Helsinki Cathedral overlooking Senate Square
photo.credit: <!-- photo: helsinki-cathedral.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 3.0 · "Lutheran Cathedral Helsinki.jpg" by Hans Hillewaert · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Lutheran_Cathedral_Helsinki.jpg -->

entries.name: Notre-Dame de Paris
entries.desc: A masterpiece of French Gothic architecture on the Île de la Cité, known for its flying buttresses, rose windows, and twin towers. Badly damaged by fire in 2019 and since restored.

photo.alt: Exterior of Notre-Dame de Paris cathedral on the Île de la Cité, Paris
photo.credit: <!-- photo: notre-dame-de-paris.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 3.0 · "Exterior of Notre-Dame de Paris, Paris 4th 007.JPG" by Moonik · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Exterior_of_Notre-Dame_de_Paris,_Paris_4th_007.JPG -->

entries.name: Sacré-Cœur
entries.desc: A white Romano-Byzantine basilica atop Montmartre, the highest point in Paris. Its travertine stone stays pale with age, and its terrace draws crowds for city views.

photo.alt: Sacré-Cœur basilica atop Montmartre, Paris
photo.credit: <!-- photo: sacre-coeur-montmartre.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 4.0 · "Basilique Sacré Cœur Montmartre façade ouest Paris 6.jpg" by Chabe01 · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Basilique_Sacr%C3%A9_C%C5%93ur_Montmartre_fa%C3%A7ade_ouest_Paris_6.jpg -->

entries.name: Strasbourg Cathedral
entries.desc: A Gothic cathedral in pink Vosges sandstone, once the world's tallest building. It is famed for its intricate facade and its Renaissance astronomical clock.

photo.alt: Strasbourg Cathedral's pink sandstone Gothic facade
photo.credit: <!-- photo: strasbourg-cathedral.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 3.0 · "Strasbourg Cathedral Exterior - Diliff.jpg" by Diliff · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Strasbourg_Cathedral_Exterior_-_Diliff.jpg -->

entries.name: Chartres Cathedral
entries.desc: One of the best-preserved examples of French High Gothic, celebrated for its extensive medieval stained glass and its labyrinth set into the nave floor.

photo.alt: West facade of Chartres Cathedral, France
photo.credit: <!-- photo: chartres-cathedral.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 3.0 · "Chartres-Cathedral.jpg" by MathKnight · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Chartres-Cathedral.jpg -->

entries.name: Basilica of Notre-Dame de Fourvière
entries.desc: A Romanesque-Byzantine basilica atop Fourvière hill, richly decorated with mosaics inside. It overlooks the city and is a prominent Lyon landmark.

photo.alt: Basilica of Notre-Dame de Fourvière overlooking Lyon
photo.credit: <!-- photo: fourviere-basilica-lyon.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 3.0 · "Fourvière Basilica, Lyon.JPG" by Lana Hussain · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Fourvi%C3%A8re_Basilica,_Lyon.JPG -->

entries.name: Reims Cathedral
entries.desc: A High Gothic cathedral where most French kings were crowned, richly ornamented with medieval sculpture including the celebrated Smiling Angel.

photo.alt: West facade of Reims Cathedral, France
photo.credit: <!-- photo: reims-cathedral.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 4.0 · "Reims Cathedral Facade 1.jpg" by Ad Meskens · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Reims_Cathedral_Facade_1.jpg -->

entries.name: Amiens Cathedral
entries.desc: The largest Gothic cathedral in France by interior volume, with the tallest complete nave in the country. Its west front carries an elaborate program of carved sculpture.

photo.alt: Amiens Cathedral's Gothic west facade
photo.credit: <!-- photo: amiens-cathedral.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 4.0 · "Amiens - Cathedrale 1.jpg" by Clément293 · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Amiens_-_Cathedrale_1.jpg -->

entries.name: Bourges Cathedral
entries.desc: A High Gothic cathedral with no transept and five radiating portals, giving the nave an unusual sense of unified space and height. Its 13th-century stained glass is among the finest in France, rivaling Chartres.

photo.alt: Bourges Cathedral's Gothic exterior
photo.credit: <!-- photo: bourges-cathedral.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 4.0 · "Bourges-Kathedrale-110-2008-gje.jpg" by Gerd Eichmann · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Bourges-Kathedrale-110-2008-gje.jpg -->

entries.name: Albi Cathedral (Cathédrale Sainte-Cécile)
entries.desc: A fortress-cathedral built in red brick following the Cathar Crusade, with virtually no exterior windows at ground level — designed to project power and permanence. Inside, the contrast with a lavishly painted Gothic interior is startling.

photo.alt: Albi Cathedral's red brick fortress exterior
photo.credit: <!-- photo: albi-cathedral.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 3.0 · "Albi cathedral - outdoor view.jpg" by Pom² · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Albi_cathedral_-_outdoor_view.jpg -->

entries.name: Cologne Cathedral (Kölner Dom)
entries.desc: A vast Gothic cathedral whose construction spanned over six centuries, with twin spires that were briefly the tallest structure in the world. It houses the Shrine of the Three Kings.

photo.alt: Cologne Cathedral's twin Gothic spires
photo.credit: <!-- photo: cologne-cathedral.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 4.0 · "Cologne Germany Exterior-view-of-Cologne-Cathedral-06.jpg" by CEphoto, Uwe Aranas · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Cologne_Germany_Exterior-view-of-Cologne-Cathedral-06.jpg -->

entries.name: Aachen Cathedral
entries.desc: The palatine chapel built by Charlemagne, the founding monument of the Holy Roman Empire. The octagonal core is among the finest Carolingian architecture surviving anywhere, and Charlemagne's throne and tomb remain inside.

photo.alt: Aachen Cathedral exterior with octagonal dome and spire, Germany
photo.credit: <!-- photo: aachen-cathedral.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 3.0 · "Aachen Germany Imperial-Cathedral-01.jpg" by CEphoto, Uwe Aranas · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Aachen_Germany_Imperial-Cathedral-01.jpg -->

entries.name: Ulm Minster (Ulmer Münster)
entries.desc: A Lutheran Gothic minster with the tallest church steeple in the world. Construction took over 500 years; the spire was not completed until 1890. Its 768-step gallery offers views across southern Germany.

photo.alt: Ulm Minster west facade and spire, Germany
photo.credit: <!-- photo: ulm-minster.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 3.0 · "Ulmer Münster-Westfassade.jpg" by Martin Kraft · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Ulmer_M%C3%BCnster-Westfassade.jpg -->

entries.name: Speyer Cathedral
entries.desc: The largest surviving Romanesque church in the world, commissioned by Holy Roman Emperor Conrad II. Its crypt holds the tombs of eight Holy Roman Emperors and German kings.

photo.alt: Speyer Cathedral's Romanesque towers and apse, Germany
photo.credit: <!-- photo: speyer-cathedral.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 3.0 · "East-front-of-Speyer-Cathedral-09.JPG" by Immanuel Giel · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:East-front-of-Speyer-Cathedral-09.JPG -->

entries.name: Matthias Church
entries.desc: A church in Budapest's Castle District, rebuilt in ornate Gothic Revival style with a colorfully tiled roof. Several Hungarian kings were crowned here.

photo.alt: Matthias Church exterior with colorfully tiled roof, Budapest
photo.credit: <!-- photo: matthias-church.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 3.0 · "Budapest Matthiaskirche 2.JPG" by Zairon · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Budapest_Matthiaskirche_2.JPG -->

entries.name: Hallgrímskirkja
entries.desc: A tall Expressionist Lutheran church whose stepped facade evokes Iceland's basalt columns. Its tower is one of the highest points in Reykjavik and offers wide views.

photo.alt: Hallgrímskirkja's stepped basalt-column facade and tower, Reykjavik
photo.credit: <!-- photo: hallgrimskirkja.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC0 · "Leif Eriksson and Hallgrimskirkja.JPG" by Ypsilon from Finland · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Leif_Eriksson_and_Hallgrimskirkja.JPG -->

entries.name: Christ Church Cathedral
entries.desc: Dublin's oldest cathedral, founded in the Viking era and later rebuilt in Gothic style. It is known for its extensive medieval crypt, one of the largest in Britain and Ireland.

photo.alt: Christ Church Cathedral exterior, Dublin
photo.credit: <!-- photo: christ-church-cathedral-dublin.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 2.0 · "Christchurch Cathedral - Dublin.jpg" by William Murphy · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Christchurch_Cathedral_-_Dublin.jpg -->

entries.name: Church of the Holy Sepulchre
entries.desc: Revered as the site of Jesus's crucifixion and burial, it is one of the holiest churches in Christianity. Six denominations share custody under a long-standing arrangement.

photo.alt: Church of the Holy Sepulchre courtyard and facade, Jerusalem
photo.credit: <!-- photo: church-of-the-holy-sepulchre.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 3.0 · "Jerusalem Holy Sepulchre BW 19.JPG" by Berthold Werner · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Jerusalem_Holy_Sepulchre_BW_19.JPG -->

entries.name: Duomo di Milano
entries.desc: One of the largest churches in the world, built in ornate Gothic style over nearly six centuries. Its marble facade bristles with spires, pinnacles, and thousands of statues.

photo.alt: Facade of Milan Cathedral (Duomo di Milano), Italy
photo.credit: <!-- photo: duomo-di-milano.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 4.0 · "Milan Cathedral (Duomo di Milano).jpg" by NahidSultan · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Milan_Cathedral_(Duomo_di_Milano).jpg -->

entries.name: Florence Cathedral (Santa Maria del Fiore)
entries.desc: A Gothic cathedral crowned by Brunelleschi's enormous brick dome, an engineering breakthrough of the Renaissance. Its exterior is clad in green, white, and pink marble.

photo.alt: Florence Cathedral's dome and campanile seen from Piazzale Michelangelo
photo.credit: <!-- photo: florence-cathedral.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 4.0 · "Florence Duomo from Michelangelo hill" by Petar Milošević · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Florence_Duomo_from_Michelangelo_hill.jpg -->

entries.name: St. Mark's Basilica
entries.desc: A Byzantine-style basilica on Piazza San Marco, opulently decorated with golden mosaics and treasures brought from Constantinople. A symbol of Venice's former wealth and power.

photo.alt: West facade of St. Mark's Basilica on Piazza San Marco, Venice
photo.credit: <!-- photo: st-marks-basilica-venice.jpg — Wikimedia Commons Public domain · "Venezia Basilica di San Marco Fassade 2" by Zairon · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Venezia_Basilica_di_San_Marco_Fassade_2.jpg -->

entries.name: Siena Cathedral (Duomo di Siena)
entries.desc: An Italian Gothic cathedral banded in black and white marble, with an elaborate inlaid marble floor and works by Donatello, Michelangelo, and Pisano.

photo.alt: Siena Cathedral's black-and-white banded marble facade
photo.credit: <!-- photo: siena-cathedral.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 3.0 · "Siena Cathedral View" by Regina Gabilondo Toscano · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Siena_Cathedral_View.JPG -->

entries.name: Pisa Cathedral
entries.desc: A medieval Romanesque cathedral in the Piazza dei Miracoli, alongside its famous leaning bell tower. It features an ornate marble facade and a carved pulpit by Giovanni Pisano.

photo.alt: Pisa Cathedral's Romanesque marble facade in the Piazza dei Miracoli
photo.credit: <!-- photo: pisa-cathedral.jpg — Wikimedia Commons Public domain · "Duomo di Pisa" by Stefan Lew · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Duomo_di_Pisa.jpg -->

entries.name: Naples Cathedral (Duomo di Napoli)
entries.desc: The main cathedral of Naples, dedicated to the city's patron Saint Januarius. It is the setting for the recurring liquefaction of his blood, drawing large crowds three times a year.

photo.alt: Main facade of Naples Cathedral
photo.credit: <!-- photo: naples-cathedral.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 3.0 · "FacciataDuomoNapoli" by Avemundi · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:FacciataDuomoNapoli.JPG -->

entries.name: Duomo di Orvieto
entries.desc: A striking Italian Gothic cathedral with a golden mosaic facade, perched on a tufa cliff. Its San Brizio Chapel holds Luca Signorelli's Last Judgment frescoes.

photo.alt: Golden mosaic facade of Orvieto Cathedral
photo.credit: <!-- photo: orvieto-cathedral.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 4.0 · "Facciata Duomo di Orvieto" by Annarita Romano (BEIC) · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Facciata_Duomo_di_Orvieto.jpg -->

entries.name: Monreale Cathedral
entries.desc: A Norman-Byzantine cathedral whose interior is entirely lined with golden mosaics depicting the Old and New Testaments — one of the greatest achievements of medieval art.

photo.alt: Exterior of Monreale Cathedral, Sicily
photo.credit: <!-- photo: monreale-cathedral.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY 2.0 · "Cathedral (Monreale) - Exterior" by Jerome Bon from Paris, France · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Cathedral_(Monreale)_-_Exterior.jpg -->

entries.name: Turin Cathedral (Duomo di Torino)
entries.desc: A Renaissance cathedral whose Chapel of the Holy Shroud houses the Shroud of Turin, the linen cloth bearing the image of a crucified man venerated as Christ's burial cloth.

photo.alt: Facade of Turin Cathedral, home of the Shroud
photo.credit: <!-- photo: turin-cathedral.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 4.0 · "Duomo di Torino Turin Cathedral" by Ambra75 · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Duomo_di_Torino_Turin_Cathedral.jpg -->

entries.name: Meiji Shrine (Meiji Jingū)
entries.desc: A major Shinto shrine dedicated to Emperor Meiji and Empress Shōken, set within a large forested park in central Tokyo. It is a popular site for traditional weddings.

photo.alt: Meiji Shrine, a Shinto shrine set within a forested park in Tokyo
photo.credit: <!-- photo: meiji-shrine-tokyo.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 3.0 · "Meiji Shrine Tokyo, Japan.jpg" by Leonard V Carlson · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Meiji_Shrine_Tokyo,_Japan.jpg -->

entries.name: Kinkaku-ji (Golden Pavilion)
entries.desc: A Zen Buddhist temple whose top two floors are covered in gold leaf, mirrored in the pond before it. The present structure is a 1955 reconstruction after arson.

photo.alt: Kinkaku-ji, the gold-leafed pavilion mirrored in Kyōko-chi pond, Kyoto
photo.credit: <!-- photo: kinkaku-ji-golden-pavilion-kyoto.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 3.0 · "Kinkaku-ji (-Temple of the Golden Pavilion-) seen across Kyōko-chi (Mirror Pond). Kita-ku, Kyoto-2.jpg" by Mstyslav Chernov · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Kinkaku-ji_(-Temple_of_the_Golden_Pavilion-)_seen_across_Ky%C5%8Dko-chi_(Mirror_Pond)._Kita-ku,_Kyoto-2.jpg -->

entries.name: Basilica of Our Lady of Guadalupe
entries.desc: The world's most-visited Catholic pilgrimage site, built on the hill where Juan Diego reported a Marian apparition in 1531. The tilma bearing the image of Our Lady is displayed in the modern basilica.

photo.alt: The modern Basilica of Our Lady of Guadalupe, Mexico City
photo.credit: <!-- photo: basilica-of-guadalupe-mexico-city.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 3.0 · "Basilica of Our Lady of Guadalupe (new).JPG" · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Basilica_of_Our_Lady_of_Guadalupe_(new).JPG -->

entries.name: Metropolitan Cathedral of Mexico City
entries.desc: The largest cathedral in the Americas, built over centuries beside the ruins of the Aztec Templo Mayor. It blends Renaissance, Baroque, and Neoclassical styles.

photo.alt: The Metropolitan Cathedral of Mexico City
photo.credit: <!-- photo: metropolitan-cathedral-mexico-city.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 4.0 · "Catedral Metropolitana de la Ciudad de México 1.jpg" by ProtoplasmaKid · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Catedral_Metropolitana_de_la_Ciudad_de_M%C3%A9xico_1.jpg -->

entries.name: Koutoubia Mosque
entries.desc: The largest mosque in Marrakech, an example of Almohad architecture whose tall minaret is a model of proportion. It influenced later towers such as Seville's Giralda.

photo.alt: The Koutoubia Mosque and its minaret, Marrakech
photo.credit: <!-- photo: koutoubia-mosque-marrakech.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 4.0 · "Koutoubia Mosque,Marrakech,Morocco.jpg" by Marcin Sochacki (Wanted) · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Koutoubia_Mosque,Marrakech,Morocco.jpg -->

entries.name: Westerkerk
entries.desc: A Dutch Renaissance Protestant church beside the Jordaan district, with the tallest church tower in Amsterdam. The painter Rembrandt is buried here.

photo.alt: The Westerkerk tower rising above Amsterdam's Jordaan district
photo.credit: <!-- photo: westerkerk-tower-amsterdam.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 4.0 · "Westerkerk tower, Amsterdam 2505.jpg" by C messier · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Westerkerk_tower,_Amsterdam_2505.jpg -->

entries.name: Nidaros Cathedral
entries.desc: Norway's national cathedral and the northernmost medieval cathedral in the world, built over the burial site of St. Olav. Norwegian monarchs are consecrated here, and it marks the end of the ancient St. Olav's Way pilgrimage.

photo.alt: West front of Nidaros Cathedral, Trondheim
photo.credit: <!-- photo: nidaros-cathedral-trondheim.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 3.0 · "Nidaros-cathedral-west-front.jpg" by Erik A. Drabløs · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Nidaros-cathedral-west-front.jpg -->

entries.name: Basilica of the Nativity
entries.desc: Built over the grotto traditionally held to be the birthplace of Jesus, it is among the oldest continuously operating churches in the world. A silver star marks the spot.

photo.alt: The Basilica (Church) of the Nativity, Bethlehem
photo.credit: <!-- photo: church-of-the-nativity-bethlehem.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY 2.0 · "The Church of The Nativity, Bethlehem, Palestine.jpg" by Ray in Manila · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:The_Church_of_The_Nativity,_Bethlehem,_Palestine.jpg -->

meta: One of the World's Oldest Continuously Operating Churches

entries.name: Cathedral of Lima
entries.desc: A colonial cathedral on Lima's Plaza Mayor, founded by conquistador Francisco Pizarro, whose remains lie inside. It blends Renaissance and Baroque elements.

photo.alt: Cathedral of Lima on Plaza Mayor
photo.credit: <!-- photo: lima-cathedral.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 3.0 · "Lima.Catedral" by Manuel González Olaechea · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Lima.Catedral.JPG -->

entries.name: Wawel Cathedral
entries.desc: Poland's national sanctuary atop Wawel Hill, where Polish kings were crowned and most are buried. Its chapels span Gothic to Baroque, and the massive Sigismund Bell is rung only on major national occasions.

photo.alt: Wawel Cathedral on Wawel Hill, Kraków
photo.credit: <!-- photo: wawel-cathedral.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 3.0 · "Kraków - Wawel Cathedral 01" by Lestat (Jan Mehlich) · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Krak%C3%B3w_-_Wawel_Cathedral_01.jpg -->

entries.name: Lisbon Cathedral (Sé de Lisboa)
entries.desc: Founded in 1147 after the Christian reconquest of Lisbon, this Romanesque cathedral has a fortress-like exterior with twin battlemented towers. Its cloister holds Roman and Moorish archaeological remains.

photo.alt: Lisbon Cathedral (Sé de Lisboa) facade
photo.credit: <!-- photo: lisbon-cathedral.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 2.0 · "Sé de Lisboa - Portugal (15225598296)" by Vitor Oliveira · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:S%C3%A9_de_Lisboa_-_Portugal_(15225598296).jpg -->

entries.name: Clérigos Tower (Torre dos Clérigos)
entries.desc: A Baroque church and bell tower designed by Nicolau Nasoni, long the tallest structure in Porto. Its tower offers panoramic views over the city and the Douro.

photo.alt: Clérigos Tower (Torre dos Clérigos), Porto
photo.credit: <!-- photo: clerigos-tower-porto.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 4.0 · "Torre dos Clérigos, Porto, 1763, 75 m de altura" by Mentxuwiki · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Torre_dos_Cl%C3%A9rigos,_Porto,_1763,_75_m_de_altura.jpg -->

entries.name: St. Basil's Cathedral
entries.desc: The iconic church on Red Square, famous for its cluster of brightly colored onion domes. Built under Ivan the Terrible, it comprises nine chapels around a central tower.

photo.alt: St. Basil's Cathedral on Red Square, Moscow
photo.credit: <!-- photo: st-basils-cathedral.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY 4.0 · "Saint Basil's Cathedral, Red Square, Moscow, Russia.jpg" by Vyacheslav Argenberg · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Saint_Basil%27s_Cathedral,_Red_Square,_Moscow,_Russia.jpg -->

entries.name: St. Isaac's Cathedral
entries.desc: A vast Neoclassical cathedral with a gilded dome that dominates the St. Petersburg skyline. Its interior is decorated with malachite and lapis lazuli columns, mosaics, and enormous paintings.

photo.alt: St. Isaac's Cathedral, St. Petersburg
photo.credit: <!-- photo: st-isaacs-cathedral.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY 2.0 · "Saint Isaac's Cathedral Saint Petersburg" by Jorge Láscar · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Saint_Isaac%27s_Cathedral_Saint_Petersburg.jpg -->

entries.name: St. Andrew's Cathedral
entries.desc: A Gothic Revival Anglican cathedral, among the largest in Southeast Asia. Its white walls owe their finish to Madras chunam, a traditional lime-and-shell plaster.

photo.alt: St. Andrew's Cathedral, Singapore
photo.credit: <!-- photo: st-andrews-cathedral-singapore.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 3.0 · "Saint Andrew's Cathedral, Singapore - 20090911" by Someformofhuman · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Saint_Andrew%27s_Cathedral,_Singapore_-_20090911.jpg -->

entries.name: Myeongdong Cathedral
entries.desc: The first Gothic church built in Korea and the seat of the Seoul Catholic archdiocese. It served as a refuge for pro-democracy activists during the 1980s.

photo.alt: Myeongdong Cathedral, Seoul
photo.credit: <!-- photo: myeongdong-cathedral.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 4.0 · "Myeongdong Cathedral 6" by kallerna · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Myeongdong_Cathedral_6.jpg -->

entries.name: Sagrada Família
entries.desc: Antoni Gaudí's unfinished basilica, a singular blend of Gothic and Art Nouveau forms with soaring organic columns. Under construction since 1882 and still incomplete.

photo.alt: Exterior of the Sagrada Família basilica, Barcelona
photo.credit: <!-- photo: sagrada-familia.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 4.0 · "Exterior of the Sagrada Família (5).jpg" by Joseolgon · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Exterior_of_the_Sagrada_Fam%C3%ADlia_(5).jpg -->

entries.name: Seville Cathedral
entries.desc: The largest Gothic cathedral in the world, built on the site of a former mosque. Its bell tower, the Giralda, was originally a minaret, and it holds a tomb attributed to Columbus.

photo.alt: Seville Cathedral with the Giralda bell tower
photo.credit: <!-- photo: seville-cathedral-giralda.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY 2.0 · "Seville Cathedral.jpg" · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Seville_Cathedral.jpg -->

entries.name: Santiago de Compostela Cathedral
entries.desc: The Romanesque cathedral believed to hold the remains of the apostle James, and the destination of the Camino de Santiago pilgrimage. Its facade is later Baroque.

photo.alt: Obradoiro façade of Santiago de Compostela Cathedral in fog
photo.credit: <!-- photo: santiago-compostela-cathedral.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 3.0 · "Obradoiro facade - Cathedral of Santiago de Compostela (5).JPG" · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Obradoiro_facade_-_Cathedral_of_Santiago_de_Compostela_(5).JPG -->

entries.name: Burgos Cathedral
entries.desc: A French-influenced Gothic cathedral known for its openwork spires and star-vaulted lantern. The medieval hero El Cid is buried beneath the crossing.

photo.alt: Burgos Cathedral facade and spires, Spain
photo.credit: <!-- photo: burgos-cathedral.jpg — Wikimedia Commons Public Domain · "Burgos Cathedral 01.jpg" by Daniel Canton (assembled by Xavax) · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Burgos_Cathedral_01.jpg -->

entries.name: León Cathedral
entries.desc: A French-influenced Gothic cathedral whose walls are almost entirely replaced by stained glass — 1,800 square metres of it, the most of any Spanish cathedral. The amber and gold light that fills the nave gives it its nickname.

photo.alt: West façade and rose window of León Cathedral
photo.credit: <!-- photo: leon-cathedral.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY 3.0 · "Cathédrale de León (Espagne).JPG" · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Cath%C3%A9drale_de_L%C3%A9on_(Espagne).JPG -->

entries.name: Mezquita-Catedral of Córdoba
entries.desc: An 8th-century Great Mosque whose forest of red-and-white striped arches was converted to a cathedral after the Christian reconquest. No other building so visibly embodies the crossing of two civilizations.

photo.alt: Red-and-white striped double arches inside the Mezquita of Córdoba
photo.credit: <!-- photo: mezquita-cordoba-arches.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY 4.0 · "Two-tiered arches at the Mosque–Cathedral of Córdoba.jpg" · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Two-tiered_arches_at_the_Mosque%E2%80%93Cathedral_of_C%C3%B3rdoba.jpg -->

entries.name: Toledo Cathedral (Catedral Primada)
entries.desc: The primatial seat of the Spanish Catholic Church, a French-influenced High Gothic cathedral rich in art. Its sacristy holds El Greco's masterpiece The Disrobing of Christ.

photo.alt: West façade and bell tower of Toledo Cathedral
photo.credit: <!-- photo: toledo-cathedral-facade.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 4.0 · "FP Toledo Cathedral 2025 - West façade.jpg" · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:FP_Toledo_Cathedral_2025_-_West_fa%C3%A7ade.jpg -->

entries.name: Hagia Sophia
entries.desc: A Byzantine engineering marvel built under Emperor Justinian, later an Ottoman mosque and a museum. Its immense dome influenced architecture for over a thousand years.

photo.alt: Front facade and minarets of Hagia Sophia, Istanbul
photo.credit: <!-- photo: hagia-sophia.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY 2.0 · "Hagia Sophia (Aya Sofya) front facade, Istanbul, Turkey (9606808746).jpg" by l0da_ralta · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Hagia_Sophia_(Aya_Sofya)_front_facade,_Istanbul,_Turkey_(9606808746).jpg -->

entries.name: Sultan Ahmed Mosque (Blue Mosque)
entries.desc: An Ottoman imperial mosque known for its cascade of domes and six minarets. Its nickname comes from the tens of thousands of blue İznik tiles lining the interior.

photo.alt: Sultan Ahmed Mosque exterior with its cascade of domes and minarets, Istanbul
photo.credit: <!-- photo: sultan-ahmed-mosque-istanbul.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 3.0 · "Exterior of Sultan Ahmed I Mosque in Istanbul, Turkey 002" by Moonik · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Exterior_of_Sultan_Ahmed_I_Mosque_in_Istanbul,_Turkey_002.jpg -->

entries.name: Sheikh Zayed Grand Mosque
entries.desc: One of the largest mosques in the world, built in white marble with dozens of domes and reflective pools. Its main prayer hall holds a vast hand-knotted carpet.

photo.alt: Exterior view of the Sheikh Zayed Grand Mosque, Abu Dhabi
photo.credit: <!-- photo: sheikh-zayed-grand-mosque.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 4.0 · "Sheikh Zayed Grand Mosque approach 09.jpg" by OasisTravelCruise · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Sheikh_Zayed_Grand_Mosque_approach_09.jpg -->

entries.name: Westminster Abbey
entries.desc: A Gothic church that has been the coronation site of English and British monarchs since 1066 and a burial place for kings, poets, and statesmen.

photo.alt: Exterior of Westminster Abbey, London
photo.credit: <!-- photo: westminster-abbey.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 4.0 · "Exterior of Westminster Abbey 20160816.jpg" by Suicasmo · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Exterior_of_Westminster_Abbey_20160816.jpg -->

entries.name: St. Paul's Cathedral
entries.desc: Sir Christopher Wren's English Baroque masterpiece, rebuilt after the Great Fire of London. Its great dome and Whispering Gallery are among the city's landmarks.

photo.alt: St. Paul's Cathedral and its great dome, London
photo.credit: <!-- photo: st-pauls-cathedral-london.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 2.0 · "St Paul's Cathedral, London (8287719102)" by Loco Steve · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:St_Paul%27s_Cathedral,_London_(8287719102).jpg -->

entries.name: Canterbury Cathedral
entries.desc: The seat of the Archbishop of Canterbury and mother church of the Anglican Communion. It became a major pilgrimage site after the murder of Thomas Becket in 1170.

photo.alt: Canterbury Cathedral's Gothic exterior, Canterbury
photo.credit: <!-- photo: canterbury-cathedral.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 4.0 · "Canterbury Cathedral - Front" by Rafa Esteve · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Canterbury_Cathedral_-_Front.jpg -->

entries.name: York Minster
entries.desc: One of the largest Gothic cathedrals in northern Europe, seat of the Archbishop of York. It holds an exceptional collection of medieval stained glass.

photo.alt: York Minster's Gothic south face, York
photo.credit: <!-- photo: york-minster.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 3.0 · "York Minster, south face - DSC07901" by Rept0n1x · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:York_Minster,_south_face_-_DSC07901.JPG -->

meta: Largest Gothic Cathedral in Northern Europe

entries.name: Salisbury Cathedral
entries.desc: An Early English Gothic cathedral with the tallest spire in Britain. It holds one of four surviving copies of the Magna Carta and a mechanical clock from 1386, the oldest still working in the world.

photo.alt: Salisbury Cathedral with the tallest spire in Britain
photo.credit: <!-- photo: salisbury-cathedral.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 4.0 · "Catedral de Salisbury, Salisbury, Inglaterra, 2014-08-12, DD 29-31 HDR" by Diego Delso · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Catedral_de_Salisbury,_Salisbury,_Inglaterra,_2014-08-12,_DD_29-31_HDR.JPG -->

entries.name: Durham Cathedral
entries.desc: A Norman cathedral on a wooded bluff above the River Wear, widely regarded as the finest Romanesque architecture in Europe. It holds the tombs of St. Cuthbert and the Venerable Bede.

photo.alt: Durham Cathedral's Norman towers above the River Wear
photo.credit: <!-- photo: durham-cathedral.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 4.0 · "Durham MMB 02 Cathedral" by mattbuck · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Durham_MMB_02_Cathedral.jpg -->

entries.name: St. Giles' Cathedral
entries.desc: The principal place of worship on Edinburgh's Royal Mile, crowned by a distinctive crown steeple. It is closely tied to the Scottish Reformation and John Knox.

photo.alt: St Giles' Cathedral and its crown steeple, Edinburgh
photo.credit: <!-- photo: st-giles-cathedral-edinburgh.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 3.0 · "St Giles Cathedral - 01.jpg" by Carlos Delgado · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:St_Giles_Cathedral_-_01.jpg -->

entries.name: St. Patrick's Cathedral
entries.desc: A Gothic Revival cathedral in Midtown Manhattan, the seat of the Roman Catholic Archdiocese of New York. Its marble facade faces Fifth Avenue opposite Rockefeller Center.

photo.alt: Gothic Revival facade of St. Patrick's Cathedral on Fifth Avenue, New York
photo.credit: <!-- photo: st-patricks-cathedral-nyc.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY 3.0 · "NYC - St. Patrick's Cathedral - Facade.jpg" by Jean-Christophe BENOIST · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:NYC_-_St._Patrick%27s_Cathedral_-_Facade.jpg -->

entries.name: Washington National Cathedral
entries.desc: A vast Neo-Gothic Episcopal cathedral used for state funerals and national services. Among its details is a stained-glass window embedded with a piece of lunar rock.

photo.alt: Neo-Gothic exterior of Washington National Cathedral
photo.credit: <!-- photo: washington-national-cathedral.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 3.0 · "Washington National Cathedral in Washington, D.C..jpg" by AgnosticPreachersKid · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Washington_National_Cathedral_in_Washington,_D.C..jpg -->

entries.name: St. Peter's Basilica
entries.desc: A Renaissance and Baroque basilica designed in part by Michelangelo and Bernini, and one of the holiest sites in Catholicism. Its dome dominates the Rome skyline.

photo.alt: Facade of St. Peter's Basilica, Vatican City
photo.credit: <!-- photo: st-peters-basilica.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC0 · "Saint Peter's Basilica facade, Rome, Italy.jpg" by Jebulon · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Saint_Peter%27s_Basilica_facade,_Rome,_Italy.jpg -->
